text: The surgeon requests a hemlock clip, infant feeding tube, and sling while separating peritoneal adhesions near the diaphragm and caudate lobe, with the inferior vena cava (IVC) visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon frees lateral adhesions of the left lobe of the liver, focusing on the flimsy left triangular ligament. The caudate lobe and inferior vena cava (IVC) are visible, along with the stomach showing a mild hiatus.

The surgeon dissects the right triangular ligament and the central part of the falciform ligament during a left lateral hepatectomy. The inferior vena cava (IVC) and a phrenic vein are visible, with caution advised due to potential bleeding from the phrenic vein.

The surgeon is dissecting the left hepatic vein, with the inferior vena cava visible adjacent to the left instrument. The left hepatic vein is being prepared for intraparenchymal transaction, as the tumor is not closely associated with the vein. The surgical field shows the segment where the final transaction will occur, with plans to use a stapler for excision.

The surgeon identifies the segment three pedicle, noting the presence of an artery and the portal vein of segment three. An instrument is prepared for division, and a Hem-o-lok clip is requested for securing the vessels.

The surgeon identifies the segment two and segment three pedicles, noting the standard anatomical location of these structures. The segment two pedicle is positioned behind the segment three pedicle, with the artery visible nearby. The surgeon plans to control all pedicles to minimize blood loss during the liver resection.

The surgeon clips the left lateral lobe hepatic artery, followed by the dissection of segment three and segment two pedicles. The hepatic vein is prepared for stapling, and the parenchyma is to be transected using a harmonic scalpel. The surgical field reveals a non-obese patient with a liver that is neither fatty nor fibrotic, facilitating clear visualization of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon performs parenchymal dissection using harmonic and clips, addressing each segment separately. If difficulty arises, staplers may be used. The dissection occurs in a relatively free part of the liver.

The surgeon is performing a liver resection, dividing the parenchyma incrementally using a harmonic scalpel. The harmonic is used in conjunction with the finger fracture method to separate liver tissue without activating the energy, allowing for precise identification of vessels. The surgeon avoids deep dissection to maintain hemostasis and delineates vessels carefully before proceeding.

The surgeon controls oozing from a vessel located in the depth of the surgical field. The vessel is now visible under direct vision, and the surgeon prepares to apply a clip using a vac applicator valve.

The surgeon uses a Powered Echelon Flex Endovascular White Stapler with a special curved beak cartridge to control inflow vessels. The stapler's beak accommodates the tissue securely, preventing vessel slippage during division.

The surgeon is dissecting the parenchyma and has reached the ligamentum venosum and the tributary of the left hepatic vein. The main left hepatic vein is exposed, and a 35 vascular load is prepared for transaction. The harmonic device is used for parenchyma fracture, and the vessel is encircled to prevent accidental slippage. The phrenic vein and other structures are spared, and the left lateral hepatectomy is completed without encircling the main portal pedicle.

The surgeon completes the hepatectomy using a Powered Echelon 35 white cartridge, designed specifically for vascular resection. The specimen is successfully removed with minimal blood loss, and the blood pressure remains stable at 100. The surgical field shows complete resection of both metastatic lesions.
